title: SEGMENT 5 — Mechanism — why afucose binds FcγRIIIa fifty times tighter

lines.text: SEGMENT 5 — Mechanism — why afucose binds FcγRIIIa fifty times tighter
Before we dive into the cell engineering, I want to spend a segment on the mechanism.

Because "afucose binds tighter" is the headline, but the molecular reason why is one of the most beautiful little pieces of structural biology in antibody engineering, and I don't want you to leave here just memorizing the fact.
I want you to feel why.
Here's the picture.
The Fc region of IgG sits on top of a receptor when it engages — picture the antibody flipped upside down, with the Fc stem pointing down into a complementary surface on the receptor.
The receptor we care about, FcγRIIIa, sits on the surface of NK cells, and it presents a binding interface that mates with the underside of the Fc.
The contact happens at the lower hinge region of the Fc and along the bottom of the CH2 domains — exactly where the Asn297 glycan lives.
Now here is the crucial structural fact.
FcγRIIIa itself is glycosylated.

The receptor has its own N-linked glycan, attached to one of its asparagines — specifically, at Asn162 of the receptor — and that receptor glycan sits right at the interface where the antibody Fc docks in.
When an antibody approaches the receptor, the receptor's Asn162 glycan and the antibody's Asn297 glycan are face to face.
They are practically touching.
Here's the analogy.
Imagine two ships trying to dock.
Each ship has fenders along its side — those soft inflatable cylinders that keep the hull from scraping the pier.
When the ships come together, the fenders meet.
Now, normally those fenders are just bumpers — they prevent damage but they don't help the ships connect.
But in our antibody-receptor docking, the fenders are doing something stranger.
They're trying to fit into each other.
Each ship's fender has a notch in the side; the other ship's fender has a peg.
They line up.
They click in.
And when they do, the ships are not just side by side — they are mechanically interlocked.
That's the Fc-Asn297-glycan and the receptor-Asn162-glycan interaction.
They form a direct sugar-sugar contact.

The antibody glycan and the receptor glycan touch each other and, when their structures are compatible, they interdigitate, forming additional non-covalent contacts that stabilize the antibody on the receptor.
Now, fucose.
The core fucose on the antibody glycan sticks out, sideways, from the bottom GlcNAc of the chitobiose core.
And it sticks out in exactly the direction that interferes with the receptor's Asn162 glycan trying to come in and touch the antibody glycan.
The fucose is, structurally, like a bumper on the bumper.
It pushes the receptor glycan away.
It prevents the sugar-sugar interdigitation.
It introduces a steric clash that weakens the docking.
Remove the fucose, and the receptor glycan can come in close.
The two glycans interdigitate.
The sugar-sugar contacts stabilize the interaction.
The binding affinity goes up by an order of magnitude or more.
And — because Fc receptors are engaged in a numbers game when they recruit NK cells to a target — that affinity boost translates into a roughly fifty-fold gain in ADCC potency.
This mechanism was worked out over several years following the Kyowa Hakko discovery, primarily through crystallography.

Groups at Genentech, at Kyowa Hakko, and in academic structural biology labs solved crystal structures of the Fc bound to FcγRIIIa, with and without the core fucose, with and without the receptor glycan.
The structures all converged on the same story: the absence of fucose lets the receptor and antibody glycans get into productive contact, and that contact stabilizes the complex.
What I want you to feel about this mechanism is how specific it is.
The fucose isn't generally messing up the docking.
It's specifically blocking one glycan from touching another.
That's why the effect is selective to FcγRIIIa — because FcγRIIIa is the Fc receptor whose glycan participates most directly in this interaction.
The other Fc receptors either don't have a glycan in the right place, or have a different glycan that doesn't interdigitate the same way.
So the afucose effect is specifically an FcγRIIIa story.
Specifically an NK-cell story.
Specifically an ADCC story.
And — this is the key clinical consequence — it is also a story that depends on the patient having functional NK cells with functional FcγRIIIa.
Because if you don't have NK cells, or if your NK cells don't bear CD16, you don't get the benefit.
We'll come back to this in Segment 17, where I'll talk about the limitations.
One more wrinkle on the mechanism, and then we move on.
The human population has a natural polymorphism in FcγRIIIa, at amino acid position 158.
Some people have a valine there — V158 — and some have a phenylalanine — F158.
The V158 variant binds IgG more tightly than the F158 variant.

People who are homozygous V/V tend to have stronger ADCC responses to therapeutic antibodies than people who are F/F homozygous, and rituximab clinical response data famously stratifies along this genotype.
Here's the punchline.
When you use an afucosylated antibody, the difference between V/V and F/F people largely disappears.
The boost in glycan-glycan interaction is so strong that even F/F patients — the weaker-binders — get good binding.
Afucose engineering equalizes the response across genotypes.

That's a beautiful clinical observation, and it's part of why afucose drugs have been so effective: they don't just boost average response, they recruit the patients who would otherwise have been undertreated.
Okay.
Now let's talk about how you actually build a cell that makes afucosylated antibody by default.
